skill.name: using-git-worktrees
skill.description: Set up isolated Git worktrees for feature work with directory selection, ignore checks, setup commands, and baseline test verification.
skill.summary: Create isolated Git workspaces before implementation work begins.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command and filesystem patterns, but most are markdown examples for a legitimate Git worktree workflow. The confirmed risk is that the skill directs agents to run package manager installs and test commands, which can execute repository code in untrusted projects.

medium_findings.title: Package Setup Commands Execute Project Code
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run npm install, cargo build, pip install, poetry install, and go mod download. These are legitimate setup steps, but they can execute scripts or fetch dependencies from untrusted projects.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly present and are expected to execute local build or install behavior. The context is legitimate development setup, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Test Commands Execute Repository-Defined Code
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents to run project test commands such as npm test, cargo test, pytest, and go test. This is useful for baseline verification but can execute arbitrary test or build hooks in an untrusted repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The test commands are clearly listed and can execute project-controlled code. The stated purpose is normal verification, which lowers concern compared with concealed execution.

low_findings.title: Git Worktree Commands Are Expected for This Skill
low_findings.description: The static command findings are mostly command examples for ls, grep, git check-ignore, git rev-parse, git worktree add, and cd. These commands match the skill purpose and do not show hidden persistence, exfiltration, or destructive behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in markdown examples and directly support Git worktree setup. No network exfiltration or obfuscation was found in these command blocks.

low_findings.title: Hidden Directory References Are Workflow Locations
low_findings.description: The skill references .worktrees and ~/.config/superpowers/worktrees as storage locations. Hidden paths deserve attention, but the text explains them as local worktree directories and does not access secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden paths are documented as worktree locations and are not used to read credentials. The home directory path is still worth noting because it writes outside the project.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Finding Was Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptography at the description and integration lines. No hash, cipher, encoding routine, or cryptographic operation appears at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found prose only, not cryptographic code or configuration. This is a clear static analysis false positive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Manager and Build Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends running setup commands that may execute lifecycle scripts, build scripts, or dependency resolution behavior controlled by the repository.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The listed commands are explicit and commonly execute project-controlled behavior. They are appropriate in trusted repositories but risky in unknown codebases.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automatic Repository Test Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends running test commands before feature work. Tests are valuable, but they can run arbitrary project scripts in malicious repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly listed in the skill. The behavior is transparent and useful, but still executes repository-controlled code.

content.user_title: Create Safe Git Worktrees for Feature Work
content.value_statement: Feature work can disturb an active workspace and hide baseline failures. This skill guides agents to create isolated Git worktrees, run setup, and verify tests before implementation.
actual_capabilities: Selects a worktree directory from existing project conventions.
Checks CLAUDE.md for worktree directory preferences.
Asks the user when no directory convention exists.
Verifies project-local worktree directories are ignored by Git.
Creates a new Git worktree and branch for feature work.
Runs detected setup and test commands for a clean baseline.
limitations: Requires a Git repository with worktree support.
May run project commands that execute repository-controlled code.
Does not clean up worktrees after feature work is complete.
Does not choose safe dependency policies for untrusted projects.

use_cases.title: Start Feature Implementation
use_cases.description: Create an isolated branch and workspace before changing code.

use_cases.title: Protect Active Local Changes
use_cases.description: Keep current files untouched while testing a separate task.

use_cases.title: Verify Baseline Before Work
use_cases.description: Run setup and tests before implementation to identify existing failures.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Worktree
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the using-git-worktrees skill to create an isolated worktree for my next feature.

prompt_templates.title: Follow Project Conventions
prompt_templates.scenario: Use existing worktree directories or documented preferences.
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a Git worktree for feature work. Follow existing project conventions and ask before choosing a new location.

prompt_templates.title: Verify Clean Baseline
prompt_templates.scenario: Create the workspace and confirm tests before implementation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a worktree for this task, run detected setup commands, then run the appropriate baseline tests.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare an Implementation Workspace
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow before executing a larger plan.
prompt_templates.prompt: Before executing the implementation plan, create an isolated worktree, verify ignore rules, install dependencies, run tests, and report the final path.

output_examples.input: Create a worktree for an auth feature.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports the selected worktree directory, the new branch name, setup results, baseline test status, and the ready workspace path.

output_examples.input: No worktree directory exists in the project.
output_examples.output: The assistant asks whether to use a project-local hidden directory or a global configuration directory before creating anything.

output_examples.input: Baseline tests fail in the new worktree.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports the failing test result and asks whether to investigate or proceed with the known failure.

best_practices: Use this skill only in repositories you trust or have already reviewed.
Confirm project-local worktree directories are ignored before creating branches.
Record baseline test failures before making implementation changes.
anti_patterns: Running setup commands in an unknown repository without review.
Continuing after failed baseline tests without user approval.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps create isolated Git worktrees for feature work and verifies the workspace before coding starts.

faq.question: Does it modify my repository?
faq.answer: It can create a branch, add a worktree, and update .gitignore when needed.

faq.question: Why does it check ignore rules?
faq.answer: It prevents project-local worktree contents from being accidentally tracked by Git.

faq.question: Does it run commands?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends Git, package setup, build, and test commands based on project files.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted code?
faq.answer: Use caution. Package install, build, and test commands can execute repository-controlled code.

faq.question: Does it remove worktrees later?
faq.answer: No. Cleanup is handled by a separate finishing workflow or by manual Git worktree commands.
